elements.sent-2411-001: Given a graph $G=(V,E)$, a connected Grundy coloring is a proper vertex coloring that can be obtained by a first-fit heuristic on a connected vertex sequence.
elements.sent-2411-002: A first-fit coloring heuristic is one that attributes to each vertex in a sequence the lowest-index color not used for its preceding neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-003: A connected vertex sequence is one in which each element, except for the first one, is connected to at least one element preceding it.
elements.sent-2411-004: The connected Grundy coloring problem consists of obtaining a connected Grundy coloring maximizing the number of colors.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we propose two integer programming (IP) formulations and a local-search enhanced biased random-key genetic algorithm (BRKGA) for the connected Grundy coloring problem.
elements.sent-2411-006: The first formulation follows the standard way of partitioning the vertices into color classes while the second one relies on the idea of representatives in an attempt to break symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-007: The BRKGA encompasses a local search procedure using a newly proposed neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-008: A theoretical neighborhood analysis is also presented.
elements.sent-2411-009: Extensive computational experiments indicate that the problem is computationally demanding for the proposed IP formulations.
elements.sent-2411-010: Nonetheless, the formulation by representatives outperforms the standard one for the considered benchmark instances.
elements.sent-2411-011: Additionally, our BRKGA can find high-quality solutions in low computational times for considerably large instances, showing improved performance when enhanced with local search and a reset mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-012: Moreover we show that our BRKGA can be easily extended to successfully tackle the Grundy coloring problem, i.e., the one without the connectivity requirements.
elements.sent-2411-013: Keywords: Discrete optimization; Graph coloring; Grundy number; Greedy heuristic; BRKGA.
elements.sent-2411-014: Graph coloring finds applications in several areas.

elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, the Grundy number and the connected Grundy number have significant applicability, as they provide a theoretical worst-case measure of solution quality.
elements.sent-2411-018: It is noteworthy that the connected Grundy coloring problem, whose optimal solution provides the connected Grundy number, is NP-hard~\citep{BenCamDouGriMorSamSil14 .}

elements.sent-2411-060: We now outline the main contributions of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-061: We propose two IP formulations for the connected Grundy coloring problem.
elements.sent-2411-062: The first one uses a standard way of partitioning the vertices into color classes while the second one employs the concept of representatives.
elements.sent-2411-063: Moreover, partially motivated by the recent successful application of a BRKGA for the Grundy coloring problem~\citep{Silvaetal2024 , we elaborate a BRKGA that employs a new local search procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Besides, we perform a theoretical analysis of the neighborhood used in our local search.
elements.sent-2411-065: To the best of our knowledge, our approaches are the first optimization methods for tackling the connected Grundy coloring problem for general graphs.

elements.sent-2411-107: The BRKGA~\citep{gonccalves2011biased is a general search metaheuristic designed to obtain high-quality solutions for challenging optimization problems.}
elements.sent-2411-108: BRKGAs have been successfully applied to several problems~\citep{resende2012,GonWas20,johnson2020,HomFonGon23,SilRibSou23,MelRibRiv23 .}
elements.sent-2411-109: We refer the reader to \citet{LonPesAndREs24a,LonPesAndRes24b for recent surveys of the BRKGA literature.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Differently from standard genetic algorithms, BRKGAs make the representation and the intensification-diversification mechanism independent of the specific problem at hand.
elements.sent-2411-111: Regarding the representation, BRKGA solutions correspond to the characterization initially proposed for random-key genetic algorithms~\citep{bean1994genetic .}
elements.sent-2411-112: Namely, the solutions are represented as a vector of randomly generated real numbers in the interval $[0, 1)$, denoted by keys.
elements.sent-2411-113: Each random-key vector encodes a single solution to the problem.
elements.sent-2411-114: When it comes to the intensification, in the mating process that generates the subsequent generation of solutions, one parent is always an elite solution, while the other is a non-elite.
elements.sent-2411-115: Besides, the elite parent has a higher probability of passing its characteristics (defined by its keys) to the offspring.
elements.sent-2411-116: The algorithm uses the parametric uniform crossover strategy~\citep{Spe91 to combine the two parent solutions when generating a new offspring.}
elements.sent-2411-117: With respect to the diversification, a BRKGA introduces new random solutions, denoted by mutants, to the population in each generation.
elements.sent-2411-118: This is an attempt to prevent early convergence by enabling the algorithm to escape from local optima areas of the solution space.
elements.sent-2411-119: As previously mentioned, BRKGA is a metaheuristic that can find high-quality solutions for the Grundy coloring problem \citep{Silvaetal2024 , but it tends to converge quickly.}
elements.sent-2411-120: To address this, we enhance the BRKGA framework considered in the study of the Grundy coloring problem \citep{Silvaetal2024 by incorporating a reset mechanism and a local search procedure: the reset starts a new search, potentially exploring a different region of the solution space, while the local search utilizes available time to examine the neighborhood of a good solution in the current population, aiming to improve the best solution found.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Figure~\ref{fig:brkga-diagram illustrates the flowchart of the local-search enhanced BRKGA framework used in our work.}
elements.sent-2411-122: The initial population, denoted as $P$ (with $p$ initial solutions), consists entirely of randomly-generated random-key vectors.
elements.sent-2411-123: At each generation, the population is sorted according to the solutions' objective values.
elements.sent-2411-124: A fraction $p_e$ of the population is denominated ELITE, while the remaining ones are classified as NON-ELITE (see Figure \ref{fig:brkga_evolve ).}
elements.sent-2411-125: The evolution phase between two generations works as follows: the elite population is entirely copied to the next generation, a percentage $p_m$ of the new population is formed by mutants, and the remaining portion is composed of new solutions generated using the intensification strategy.
elements.sent-2411-126: If a new best solution is found in a generation, then a local search (see Section~\ref{sec:localsearch ) is performed on a number $b$ of solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-127: After that, $b$ random-key vectors corresponding to the solutions obtained in the local search are created to replace the $b$ worst solutions of that generation.
elements.sent-2411-128: If $g_{lim}$ generations pass without improving the best-known solution, then the population is restarted keeping only the best-known solution for the next generation.

elements.sent-2411-131: A primary component of a BRKGA implementation is its decoder, a deterministic algorithm responsible for mapping the random-key vector to a feasible solution for the problem at hand and computing its objective value.
elements.sent-2411-132: In addition to the decoder, the BRKGA requires and is guided by the following parameters: the population size ($p$), the size of the elite population ($p_e$), the size of the mutant population ($p_m$), the elite inheritance probability ($\rho_e$), and the number of generations ($n_g$) or some other stopping criterion (such as maximum time), and the maximum number of generations without improvements before restarting ($g_{lim}$).

elements.sent-2411-135: The decoder defines the vertex coloring order considering the $i$-th key in the random-key vector representing a solution as the priority of the $i$-th vertex to be selected while respecting the connectivity constraint of the problem.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption {ColorSequence ($G$, $S$, $colors$, $v$, $nPosition$, $fPosition$) } \label{alg:colorsequence} $S' \leftarrow move(S, v, nPosition)$\;\label{cs:move} $colors' \leftarrow copyColors(colors, fPosition)$\;\label{cs:copy} $checkChange \leftarrow FALSE$\;\label{cs:initCheck} \ForEach{$p \in (fPosition,|V|]$}{\label{cs:initLoop} \If{$checkChange = FALSE$}{\label{cs:initNotChange} \If{$p > nPosition$ or $S' \notin N[v]$}{\label{cs:checkInterval} $colors'[S'] \leftarrow colors[S']$\;\label{cs:copyOutCritical} } \Else{\label{cs:initcheckN} $colors'[S'] \leftarrow$ Color-Vertex$(G,S', colors')$\;\label{cs:tryColor} \If{$colors'[S'] \neq colors[S']$}{ $checkChange \leftarrow TRUE$\; } }\label{cs:endcheckN} }\label{cs:endNotChange} \Else{\label{cs:alrChange} $colors'[S'] \leftarrow$ Color-Vertex$(G,S', colors')$\;\label{cs:alrChangeColor} } }\label{cs:endLoop} \Return $S', \max_{v \in V}colors', colors'$\;\label{cs:return}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption {LocalSearch ($G$, $x$) } \label{alg:decoder_con_grundy_1opt} $colors, max \leftarrow $ Decoder-Connected-Grundy($G$, $x$)\; \label{deccopt:initialSolution} $S \leftarrow sequence(x)$\; \label{deccopt:seq} $hasIncrease \leftarrow \mathit{TRUE}$ \; \label{deccopt:checkControl} \While{$hasIncrease = \mathit{TRUE}$}{ \label{deccopt:iniWhileLoop} $hasIncrease \leftarrow \mathit{FALSE}$\; \label{deccopt:resethasincrease} $f_{S}, fc_{S}, p_{S} \leftarrow computeFirstNeighborAndVerticesPosition(S)$\; \label{deccopt:auxStructs} \ForEach{$v \in V$ while $hasIncrease = \mathit{FALSE}$}{ \label{deccopt:iniForLoop} \ForEach{$u \in N(v)$ while $hasIncrease = \mathit{FALSE}$}{ \label{deccopt:iniInForLoop} \If{$p_{S}[u] < p_{S}[v]$ and $canMoveLeft(G, S, f_{S}, fc_{S}, p_{S}, u, v)$}{ \label{deccopt:checkCriticalPositionBefore} $S', colors', max' \leftarrow $ ColorSequence($G$, $S$, $colors$, $v$, $p_{S}[u]$, $p_{S}[u]-1$)\; \label{deccopt:reconstructSolBefore} \If{$max < max'$}{\label{deccopt:checkBefore} $S, max, colors \leftarrow S', max', colors'$\; $hasIncrease \leftarrow \mathit{TRUE}$\; }\label{deccopt:updateBefore} } \If{$p_{S}[u] > p_{S}[v]$ and $canMoveRight(G, S, f_{S}, fc_{S}, p_{S}, u, v)$}{\label{deccopt:checkCriticalPositionAfter} $S', colors', max' \leftarrow $ ColorSequence($G$, $S$, $colors$, $v$, $p_{S}[u]$, $p_{S}[v]-1$)\; \label{deccopt:reconstructSolAfter} \If{$max < max'$}{\label{deccopt:checkAfter} $S, max, colors \leftarrow S', max', colors'$\; $hasIncrease \leftarrow \mathit{TRUE}$\; }\label{deccopt:updateAfter} } }\label{deccopt:endInForLoop} }\label{deccopt:endForLoop} }\label{deccopt:endWhileLoop} \Return $S$\; \label{deccopt:return}

elements.sent-2411-307: Instances (a)-(c) were produced using the graph generator ggen~\citep{ggen by \citet{MelQueSan21 .}}

elements.sent-2411-310: Instances (d) refer to the complements of the bipartite graphs specified in (c).

elements.sent-2411-314: Notably, each graph class entails 80 instances.

elements.sent-2411-464: In this paper, we considered the connected Grundy coloring problem.
elements.sent-2411-465: We presented the first integer programming formulations for the problem: a standard formulation and a formulation by representatives.
elements.sent-2411-466: Additionally, we proposed a local-search enhanced BRKGA.
elements.sent-2411-467: To the best of our knowledge, our approaches are the first general optimization methods for the connected Grundy coloring problem.
elements.sent-2411-468: Moreover, we performed a theoretical analysis of the neighborhood employed in the local search procedure.
elements.sent-2411-469: We carried out extensive experiments to evaluate the performance of the approaches.
elements.sent-2411-470: The computational results indicate that the integer programming formulations are suitable for instances with up to 30 vertices.
elements.sent-2411-471: The formulation by representatives performs better than the standard formulation, particularly for denser instances.
elements.sent-2411-472: We remark that a similar behavior was observed for the Grundy coloring problem \citep{Silvaetal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-473: Additionally, in graphs with up to 30 vertices, 178 optima were found out of the 240 tested instances (74.16\%), although only 132 were directly solved to optimality by the formulations.
elements.sent-2411-474: This suggests that the formulations effectively find high-quality solutions, and even optima ones, but they face challenges to prove optimality.
elements.sent-2411-475: The tests for the connected Grundy problem using BRKGA-B and BRKGA+R+LS demonstrated that these approaches perform well, achieving good-quality solutions.
elements.sent-2411-476: They were used to successfully tackle instances with up to 500 vertices.
elements.sent-2411-477: The results indicate that BRKGA+R+LS generally outperforms BRKGA-B in terms of the average total number of colors obtained, particularly for random graphs.
elements.sent-2411-478: The two BRKGA variants reached the same best solutions for most of the instances, except in 48 cases.
elements.sent-2411-479: Out of these, BRKGA+R+LS achieved the best results for 31, where the reset and local search address fast convergence, facilitating a broader exploration of the search space and leading to equal or improved solutions.
elements.sent-2411-480: Our study also demonstrated that combining a reset mechanism and local search with the BRKGA is effective for the Grundy coloring problem, achieving new best-known solutions for 36 instances.
elements.sent-2411-481: Additionally, BRKGA+R+LS exhibited robust performance, surpassing BRKGA-G in both average and best solutions, particularly for the random and bipartite graph classes.
elements.sent-2411-482: Overall, the results emphasize the importance of combining reset and local search to address the BRKGA's fast convergence for these problems, enabling a broader exploration of the search space.
